we can make the procedure more flexible by enlarging the feature space using basis expansions such as polynomials or splines (Chapter 5)

In this chapter we describe generalizations of linear decision boundaries for classification. Optimal separating hyperplanes are introduced in Chapter 4 for the case when two classes are linearly separable.

We can represent the optimization problem (12.9) and its solution in a special way that only involves the input features via inner products. We do this directly for the transformed feature vectors h(xi). We then see that for particular choices of h, these inner products can be computed very cheaply.
So both (12.19) and (12.20) involve h(x) only through inner products. In fact, we need not specify the transformation h(x) at all, but require only knowledge of the kernel function
Notice that this has the same form as both the radial basis function expansion and a regularization estimate, discussed in Chapters 5 and 6.
Radial basis: K(x, x′) = exp(−γ∥x −x′∥2),
So both (12.19) and (12.20) involve h(x) only through inner products. In fact, we need not specify the transformation h(x) at all, but require only knowledge of the kernel function
that computes inner products in the transformed space. K should be a symmetric positive (semi-) definite function; see Section 5.8.1.

we can make the procedure more flexible by enlarging the feature space using basis expansions such as polynomials or splines (Chapter 5). Generally linear boundaries in the enlarged space achieve better training-class separation, and translate to nonlinear boundaries in the original space.

Generally linear boundaries in the enlarged space achieve better training-class separation, and translate to nonlinear boundaries in the original space.

The support vector classifier described so far finds linear boundaries in the input feature space. As with other linear methods, we can make the procedure more flexible by enlarging the feature space using basis expansions such as polynomials or splines (Chapter 5).

In Chapter 4 we discussed a technique for constructing an optimal separating hyperplane between two perfectly separated classes. We review this and generalize to the nonseparable case, where the classes may not be separable by a linear boundary.
Here we cover extensions to the nonseparable case, where the classes overlap.

with nonzero coefficients α̂i only for those observations i for which the constraints in (12.16) are exactly met (due to (12.14)). These observations are called the support vectors, since β̂ is represented in terms of them alone.
Among these support points, some will lie on the edge of the margin (ξ̂i = 0), and hence from (12.15) and (12.12) will be characterized by 0 < α̂i < C; the remainder (ξ̂i > 0) have α̂i = C.
From (12.14) we can see that any of these margin points (0 < α̂i, ξ̂i = 0) can be used to solve for β0, and we typically use an average of all the solutions for numerical stability.

In this chapter we describe generalizations of linear decision boundaries for classification.
Optimal separating hyperplanes are introduced in Chapter 4 for the case when two classes are linearly separable. Here we cover extensions to the nonseparable case, where the classes overlap.
The support vector classifier described so far finds linear boundaries in the input feature space.
Generally linear boundaries in the enlarged space achieve better training-class separation, and translate to nonlinear boundaries in the original space.

In Chapter 4 we discussed a technique for constructing an optimal separating hyperplane between two perfectly separated classes. We review this and generalize to the nonseparable case, where the classes may not be separable by a linear boundary.
which produces nonlinear boundaries by constructing a linear boundary in a large, transformed version of the feature space

The support vector classifier described so far finds linear boundaries in the input feature space. As with other linear methods, we can make the procedure more flexible by enlarging the feature space using basis expansions such as polynomials or splines (Chapter 5).
Generally linear boundaries in the enlarged space achieve better training-class separation, and translate to nonlinear boundaries in the original space.

that computes inner products in the transformed space. K should be a symmetric positive (semi-) definite function; see Section 5.8.1.
Three popular choices for K in the SVM literature are
dth-Degree polynomial: K(x, x′) = (1 + ⟨x, x′⟩)d,

Radial basis: K(x, x′) = exp(−γ∥x −x′∥2),
Neural network: K(x, x′) = tanh(κ1⟨x, x′⟩+ κ2).
